title: Tell a Story People Remember
developmental_frame: At 9–10, children love stories and can retell events, but they tend to ramble — every detail feels equally important and the point gets lost. 'Beginning, middle, end' is a handle they can genuinely grasp at this age, and they light up performing for a real audience. Keep it spoken and playful; writing long pieces is a different, harder skill best kept small here.
authentic_value_meaning: Value at this age is a listener who's genuinely gripped — leaning in, laughing, remembering your story afterward. It's the first taste of holding an audience, usually family or friends, with something you experienced or imagined.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child tells a real story to a real listener and finds out whether it landed — did they laugh, gasp, remember it later? A live audience reacting (or not) is powerfully motivating at this age; performing and being enjoyed feels great, and the flat moments teach fast.

artifact: A short told-or-recorded story (2–3 minutes) about something real that happened, with a clear beginning, middle, and end, delivered to a real listener.
success_criteria: The story has a clear beginning, middle, and end a listener can follow.
The child left out boring bits and kept the interesting part.
A real listener could retell the main thing that happened afterward.

lessons.title: Beginning, middle, end
lessons.concept: Every good story has three parts: a beginning (who and where), a middle (what happened — usually a problem or surprise), and an end (how it turned out). That shape is what makes a story feel like a story.
lessons.real_example: 'I went to the park (beginning), a dog stole my sandwich (middle), and the owner bought me an ice cream to say sorry (end).' Simple — but it's a real story because it has all three.
lessons.apply_task: Think of something real that happened to you. Say it in just three sentences: beginning, middle, end.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which part was hardest to pin down? Often it's the middle — the actual interesting thing.
lessons.feedback_task: Tell your three-sentence version to someone. Ask if it felt complete or like something was missing.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a younger child turn something that happened to them into a beginning-middle-end.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Use your own real memory here — a story that happened to *you* beats any made-up one a computer could write, because you actually lived it.
success_criteria: A real event is told in a clear three-part shape.

lessons.title: Keep the good part
lessons.concept: A rambly story buries the good bit under boring details. Find the most interesting moment — the surprise, the problem, the funny part — and make sure it shines. Cut the rest.
lessons.real_example: Nobody needs to hear what you had for breakfast before the dog stole your sandwich. Skip to the good part — the theft!
lessons.apply_task: Take your story and underline the single most interesting moment. Cross out two boring details that don't help it.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Was it hard to cut things? We get attached to details even when they slow the story down.
lessons.feedback_task: Tell the trimmed version to someone. Did it feel snappier than before?
lessons.teach_back_task: Listen to a friend's story and gently help them spot the one boring bit to cut.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: You decide what the 'good part' is — it's your story and your sense of what's exciting. That judgement is yours to build, not a computer's to hand you.
success_criteria: The most interesting moment is identified and at least two boring details cut.

lessons.title: Use your voice and face
lessons.concept: The same words are boring in a flat voice and thrilling with expression. Slowing down at the exciting bit, going quiet, making a face — that's how you pull a listener in.
lessons.real_example: Watch how a good storyteller drops their voice to a whisper right before the scary part. You lean in without even deciding to.
lessons.apply_task: Tell your story out loud twice — once totally flat, once with your best voice and faces. Feel the difference.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which version would *you* rather listen to? What did your voice do at the exciting moment?
lessons.feedback_task: Perform the expressive version for someone and watch their face. Did they react more than to the flat one?
lessons.teach_back_task: Show a family member the flat-vs-expressive trick with a single sentence.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is all you — your real voice and face. It's a performing skill, and no screen can do the performing for you.
success_criteria: The story was performed with deliberate voice/expression changes at key moments.

lessons.title: Tell it for real
lessons.concept: Now put it together and tell your whole story to a real audience. The test of a story is simple: can they remember what happened afterward?
lessons.real_example: If your friend is still talking about your sandwich-thief dog the next day, your story worked. Being remembered is the goal.
lessons.apply_task: Tell (or record) your full 2–3 minute story for a real listener, using your three-part shape, the good part, and your best expression.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Where did your listener react most? That's the part that worked — remember what you did there.
lessons.feedback_task: A little later, ask your listener to retell your story. What they remember (and forget) shows you what landed.
lessons.teach_back_task: Coach someone else through telling their story to a real audience.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Tell it to a real person, not a device. The whole reward — a human remembering your story — only happens with a real listener.
success_criteria: A complete story was told to a real audience.
The listener could retell the main event afterward.
